startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: Work through this clinical case to test your understanding of key concepts in respiratory anatomy. Make diagnostic and management decisions based on the clinical scenario.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You made the correct clinical decisions at each step. You demonstrated strong understanding of the underlying concepts. Well done!
You showed some understanding but chose a suboptimal path. Review the feedback provided and try again to reinforce your learning.
Your clinical reasoning needs strengthening in this area. Review the source material carefully, paying attention to the key concepts highlighted, then attempt the case again.

params.text:
Case: Child with Choking

A 3-year-old child is brought to the emergency department after choking on a peanut 2 hours ago. He has a persistent cough, unilateral wheeze on the right side, and decreased breath sounds on the right. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation of the right lung with mediastinal shift to the left on expiration.

Key concept: The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, making it the common site for foreign body aspiration.

branchingQuestion.question: Why has the peanut most likely lodged in the right bronchus? What anatomical feature explains this?

alternatives.text: The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The right bronchus being wider, shorter, and more vertical makes it the path of least resistance for aspirated foreign bodies.

alternatives.text: The left bronchus is blocked by the aortic arch, diverting objects to the right

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: While the aortic arch crosses over the left bronchus, the primary reason for right-sided aspiration is the right bronchus being wider, shorter, and more vertical.

alternatives.text: The carina deflects all objects to the right side by default

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The carina (T4/T5 bifurcation) does not preferentially deflect objects. The right bronchus anatomy (wider, shorter, more vertical) is the key factor.

params.text:
Bronchoscopy Findings

Rigid bronchoscopy confirms a peanut lodged in the right main bronchus. It is successfully removed. The pulmonologist notes the trachea is approximately 10-11 cm long, extending from C6 to T4/T5 where it bifurcates at the carina.

Concept: The right lung has 3 lobes and 10 bronchopulmonary segments. The left lung has 2 lobes. The trachea has 16-20 C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings that are open posteriorly.

branchingQuestion.question: A chest drain may be needed if complications develop. Where should the needle be inserted relative to the rib to avoid the neurovascular bundle?

alternatives.text: Over the upper (superior) border of the lower rib

feedback.title: Correct!
feedback.subtitle: The intercostal neurovascular bundle (vein, artery, nerve) runs along the inferior border of each rib. Insert over the superior border of the lower rib to avoid injury.

alternatives.text: Along the lower (inferior) border of the upper rib

feedback.title: Dangerous!
feedback.subtitle: The neurovascular bundle runs in the costal groove along the INFERIOR border of the rib. Inserting here risks damaging the intercostal vessels and nerve.

alternatives.text: Through the middle of the intercostal space — the bundle is in the posterior chest only

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: The neurovascular bundle runs along the entire inferior border of the rib, not just posteriorly. Always insert over the SUPERIOR border of the lower rib.

params.text:
Clinical Resolution

The peanut is removed successfully. The child recovers fully with no complications. Parents are counselled about choking hazards in young children.

Learning points:

  • Trachea: 10-11 cm, C6 to T4/T5, bifurcates at carina
  • Right bronchus: wider, shorter, more vertical — common aspiration site
  • Right lung: 3 lobes, 10 segments; Left: 2 lobes
  • Trachea: 16-20 C-shaped cartilage rings, open posteriorly
  • Chest drain: over superior border of lower rib
